fragments.text: Cox incident label 'Magic dresses': the garment that IS the transformation, often delivered inside a nut or almond, or struck out of a pigsty wall with a stick.
Cox incident labels 'Lost shoe' and 'Shoe marriage test': identity proven not by face but by whether a small hollow object fits the body exactly.
fragments.source: Cox 1893 entries 1-11 passim — labels 'Lost shoe', 'Shoe marriage test'

fragments.text: Cox incident label 'Mutilated foot': the false claimant cuts her own foot to force it into the shoe — passing the fit test by paying flesh.
Cox incident label 'False bride': a substitute rides away wearing the heroine's place; the swap is invisible to the bridegroom until something small betrays it.
Cox incident label 'Animal witness': a raven, cock, magpie, dove or crow announces the hidden truth — animals see the swap that people miss.
Cox incident label 'Threefold flight': the heroine appears transformed, then must escape before being touched — three timed crossings back over the same threshold.
Cox incident label 'Pitch trap': the exit smeared sticky so that crossing the boundary costs a shoe — the threshold itself made adhesive.
Cox incident label 'Ear cornucopia': a whole meal kept inside a living animal's ear — reach in and pull out food.
Cox incident label 'Revivified bones': the slain helper's bones, collected and buried or smoked forty days, convert into dresses, treasure, or the animal alive again.
Cox's layered wearing: magic dress worn under husk, rags over magic dress, galoshes over gold shoes, pigskin cloak over princess — splendor kept as the hidden inner layer.
fragments.source: Cox 1893 entries 11, 58, 62, 64 — labels 'Heroine disguise', 'Magic dress worn under husk'

fragments.text: Cox incident label 'Token objects thrown': water, towel, comb flung behind the fleeing heroine become obstacles between her and the pursuer.
Cox incident labels 'Slaying of helpful animal' and 'Eating taboo': the helper must be killed for its gift to convert, but the heroine must not eat it.
fragments.source: Cox 1893 entries 3, 5, 52, 54, 68 — labels 'Slaying of helpful animal', 'Eating taboo'

fragments.text: Catarina carries the aunt's nut to her own room before breaking it: inside the shell, a whole silk dress; later an almond crushed open holds gold slippers.
Thirsty brother meets rain-filled hoofprints in sequence — horse, ox, buffalo, bear, pig, wolf — each drink a different animal shape; he drinks from the sheep's print, becomes ram.
The little black lamb tells the hungry girl to put her finger in its ear: bread inside one ear, cheese inside the other.
The prince hews the pigeon-house to pieces with an axe to catch the fled girl — no one inside; she is already back in ashes by the hazel tree.
Pigeons whir through the kitchen window to sort lentils out of ashes — 'the good into the pot, the bad into the crop' — a fork enacted beak by beak.
The king's son smears the whole staircase with pitch; at the third midnight flight her left slipper stays stuck on the step, small and all golden.
The step-sister cuts off her toe, forces the foot into the shoe, swallows the pain; on the hazel-tree two pigeons cry 'blood within the shoe'.
In the bull's left ear lies a folded cloth; spread on the grass it serves up dishes, wine, mead, and sweet cake.
Katie dons a cloak built of wooden lath strips over herself; climbing the palace stairs it clatters so loudly the prince comes out to look.
The henwife clips a piece from Trembling's old clothes, puts on the cloak of darkness, and wishes: that moment the whitest robes in the world exist, grown from the swatch.
The swallowing whale casts Trembling out on the sand with each tide, three times; it can only be killed by a silver bullet in the reddish-brown spot under its breast-fin.
Mjadveig takes off her dress and hands it over; with the exchange of dresses the queen's charm exchanges faces too — the garment carries the identity.
fragments.source: Árnason, tr. Powell and Magnússon 1866, 'The Story of Mjadveig, Daughter of Mani'

fragments.text: In the Epic of Gilgamesh, the snake steals the plant of youth and immediately sheds its skin, sliding away renewed, leaving the husk behind.
fragments.source: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI (Mesopotamia, c. 2100 BCE)

fragments.text: Ovid's Marsyas, flayed by Apollo, cries 'Why do you tear me from myself?' — his whole body one wound, the skin peeled away entire.
In Ovid's Metamorphoses, thin bark closes over Daphne's breast as she runs; skin hardens to rind, hair to leaves, the girl still warm underneath.
In Orkney selkie tales, the seal-wife finds her hidden sealskin in the locked chest, slips it on, and returns to the sea forever.
In the Noh play Hagoromo, a fisherman withholds the celestial maiden's feather robe; without it she cannot ascend — the garment is the border between worlds.
In Barrie's Peter Pan, Peter's shadow, torn off by a slamming window, is rolled up in a drawer until Wendy sews it back to his heels.
In Heda's Dutch vanitas still lifes, a lemon's peel hangs off the table edge in one unbroken spiral, pale flesh exposed, rind curling into shadow.
fragments.source: Willem Claesz Heda, banketje still lifes (Netherlands, 1630s)

fragments.text: Gijsbrechts's 1670 trompe-l'oeil shows only the back of a painting — stretcher, canvas, inventory tag; turning it over would reveal just another back.
fragments.source: Cornelis Norbertus Gijsbrechts, Reverse of a Framed Painting (Copenhagen, 1670)

fragments.text: Seventeenth-century quodlibet trompe-l'oeil engravings render printed sheets whose corners curl up with false shadows, forever promising a liftable page and another image beneath.
fragments.source: Quodlibet / letter-rack trompe-l'oeil prints (Flanders and Netherlands, 17th c.)

fragments.text: Magritte's The Human Condition: a canvas on an easel exactly continues the landscape behind it — removing the painting would remove the view itself.
In Perrault's Donkeyskin, the princess flees wrapped in a donkey's hide, sun-and-moon dresses hidden beneath; a ring baked into a cake unmasks her.
In Holbein's The Ambassadors, a grey smear across the floor resolves into a skull only from one oblique standpoint; step aside and death vanishes.
fragments.source: Hans Holbein the Younger, The Ambassadors (London, 1533)

fragments.text: A matryoshka splits at the waist to release a smaller identical self, again and again, until the last doll — solid wood, unopenable.
fragments.source: Russian matryoshka, Zvyozdochkin and Malyutin (Sergiev Posad, 1890s)

fragments.text: The German advent calendar rations revelation: twenty-four paper doors, each hiding one small picture, each forbidden until its numbered day arrives.
fragments.source: German Adventskalender, Gerhard Lang's printed versions (Munich, c. 1908)

fragments.text: Victorian tunnel books stage the 1843 Thames Tunnel as stacked cut-paper layers seen through a peephole — a whole tunnel compressed into a paper accordion.
Apian's Astronomicum Caesareum (1540) builds the heavens from stacked rotating paper discs — volvelles — layers of the cosmos turned by a silk thread.
Pollock's toy theaters — 'penny plain, twopence coloured' — shrink Drury Lane to a tabletop proscenium; cardboard actors slide on wires into a miniature storm.
In the Tale of Genji, the lady slips free of her robe to escape Genji, who keeps the shed garment and names her Utsusemi — cicada shell.
fragments.source: Murasaki Shikibu, Genji Monogatari, ch. 3 'Utsusemi' (Japan, c. 1008)

fragments.text: The arm reliquary of Charlemagne shapes its jeweled case as the arm it contains — the body's outside remade in silver around the bone inside.
fragments.source: Arm reliquary of Charlemagne, Aachen Cathedral Treasury (1481)

fragments.text: A Noh ko-omote mask, tilted down, grieves; tilted up, it smiles — one carved wooden face holding both expressions, released by angle alone.
fragments.source: Noh ko-omote mask tradition (Japan, from 14th c.)

fragments.text: Vesalius-era fugitive sheets: anatomical engravings with paper flaps — lift the chest door of the printed man and his viscera open layer by layer.
fragments.source: Anatomical fugitive sheets, Heinrich Vogtherr and successors (Strasbourg, 1538 onward)

fragments.text: Houdon's écorché (1767): a flayed man stands calmly, arm outstretched, every muscle exposed — the skin gone, the inside now the permanent outside.
The Archimedes Palimpsest: tenth-century geometry scraped from parchment and overwritten as a prayer book — the older text ghosting beneath, recoverable by raking light.
Cornell's Medici Slot Machine: a Renaissance boy stands behind crosshair-etched glass among compartments of marbles and jacks — a princeling sealed in a penny arcade.
fragments.source: Joseph Cornell, Medici Slot Machine (New York, 1942)

fragments.text: In Carroll's Alice in Wonderland, behind a low curtain waits a fifteen-inch door; through it Alice sees the loveliest garden — at her size, unreachable.
fragments.source: Lewis Carroll, Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, ch. 1 (Oxford, 1865)

fragments.text: In Carroll's Through the Looking-Glass, the mirror over the mantel softens to bright silvery mist, and Alice climbs through the melting glass into the reversed room.
fragments.source: Lewis Carroll, Through the Looking-Glass, ch. 1 (Oxford, 1871)

fragments.text: Borges's Aleph: a small iridescent sphere on a cellar stair, two or three centimetres wide, containing every point of the universe seen at once.
fragments.source: Jorge Luis Borges, El Aleph (Buenos Aires, 1945)

fragments.text: Calvino's Olinda, in Invisible Cities, grows in concentric rings like a trunk; at the center a new tiny Olinda swells, pushing the old outward.
In Gogol's The Nose, the barber finds Kovalyov's nose baked inside his breakfast loaf; days later it rides a carriage in a state councillor's uniform.
In Grimm's Allerleirauh, the princess folds three dresses — gold as sun, silver as moon, glittering as stars — into a nutshell, then cloaks herself in a thousand furs.
Méliès pumps his own duplicate head with bellows in The Man with the Rubber Head; it swells to fill the room, then bursts.
fragments.source: The Man with the Rubber Head (1901)

fragments.text: Keaton steps into the movie screen in Sherlock Jr.; each cut strands him on a new set — garden, cliff edge, lion den — the world reshuffling underfoot.
Rings of light climb the seated machine-man in Metropolis as Maria's stolen face forms over the chrome, one luminous stratum at a time.
Dorothy opens the sepia farmhouse door in The Wizard of Oz and the doorway becomes a window flooded with Technicolor — the dull inside framing a saturated elsewhere.
Shrunk below doll size in The Incredible Shrinking Man, Scott duels a cellar spider with a sewing pin and beds down inside a matchbox.
In Karel Zeman's Invention for Destruction, live actors sail seas hatched like Victorian engravings — etched flat lines and breathing flesh sharing one continuous world.
In The Colour of Pomegranates, crushed pomegranates bleed dark juice through white cloth, the stain spreading into the shape of an ancient kingdom.
Honey's first henshin in Cutie Honey: clothes burst away in a ring of light and a new self snaps on — the ur-template of magical-girl molting.
Norstein's hedgehog steps into the fog bank in Hedgehog in the Fog and half-vanishes; a white horse surfaces from the vapor like a developing photograph.
In The Thing, Norris's chest splits open into a toothed mouth mid-defibrillation; later his severed head sprouts crab legs and walks away.
A pencil-drawn hand reaches out of the comic panel in a-ha's Take On Me and pulls the girl through; inside, she is rotoscope lines too.
Peter Gabriel's face rebuilt frame by frame in Sledgehammer: fruit orbits his pixilated head, plums for eyes, a mask assembling and unassembling itself.
Tetsuo's arm in Akira regrows from cables and scrap, then his whole body balloons into a flesh avalanche that swallows what he loves.
Svankmajer's taxidermy rabbit in Alice tears itself off its museum mount, leaking sawdust from the split seam, and pins its own belly shut.
Usagi's henshin in Sailor Moon: ribbons of light wrap her silhouette and harden into uniform, nails first — the same rehearsed molt every episode.
Gromit, kneeling on a runaway toy train in The Wrong Trousers, lays each curve of track from a box an instant before the wheels need it.
Ghost in the Shell opens with a body born in layers: metal skeleton dipped, muscle knit on, then a skin sheath that flakes away as Kusanagi wakes.
In Neon Genesis Evangelion, Unit-01's purple armor is revealed as restraints; berserk, the faceplate cracks and a living eye and bleeding jaw push through.
Neo touches the cracked mirror in The Matrix; it goes liquid, crawls up his arm like chrome syrup, and pours down his throat.
At the climax of Adolescence of Utena, Utena transforms into a pink car; Anthy grips the wheel and drives her friend's body out of the world.
A waist-high door behind a filing cabinet in Being John Malkovich opens on a mud-slick tunnel into an actor's skull; fifteen minutes later you drop beside the turnpike.
In Chris Cunningham's All Is Full of Love, assembly arms plate a robot Björk together — porcelain panels over wet machinery — while she kisses her own duplicate.
In Spirited Away, Chihiro's parents bulge into pigs mid-bite, cheeks splitting their human clothes, while she stands at the tunnel mouth between worlds.
The door dial in Howl's Moving Castle: four wedges of paint, four cities; turn the knob and the same doorframe opens somewhere else.
In Paprika, the villain pins the dream-girl to a table and peels her skin back like a wetsuit, revealing Dr. Chiba asleep inside her own alter ego.
The little door in Coraline is bricked up by day; at night the bricks are gone and a ribbed, breathing tunnel glows toward a copied home.
At the end of Under the Skin, the alien peels her human surface open from the back and holds her own blinking face in her hands, regarding it.
In Song of the Sea, Saoirse buttons on the white selkie coat her father keeps locked in a chest, and the sea finally accepts her.
At kataware-doki in Your Name — the twilight seam between day and night — Taki and Mitsuha finally stand on the same crater rim, until the light closes.
Miles' leap of faith in Into the Spider-Verse: he falls upward through Ben-Day dots and shattering glass, the frame rate doubling as the suit becomes his.
Gushers Fruit Heads: eating the fruit snack instantly turns the eater's head into the matching fruit — a normal kid's body topped by an enormous, unmistakable fruit head.
fragments.source: Gushers — Fruit Heads (1990s), record grade B

fragments.text: Capri Sun Disrespectoids: how a kid handles the pouch determines the transformation — bend, squeeze or mishandle it and the body becomes a grotesquely literal embodiment of that exact abuse.
fragments.source: Capri Sun — Disrespectoids / Respect the Pouch (2009–), record grade B

fragments.text: Skittles Touch: anything Tim touches turns to Skittles — his office desk disintegrates into candy while coworkers happily eat the evidence and he despairs at his own contagious hand.
Evian Baby & Me: adults see their inner selves as baby reflections moving in perfect sync — a dancer in the street mirrored by an identically dressed baby in the shop window.
fragments.source: Evian — Baby & Me (2013), record grade A

fragments.text: Old Spice The Man Your Man Could Smell Like: one unbroken direct-address monologue holds steady while set, props and scale transform around the spokesman — ending seated backward on a horse.
fragments.source: Old Spice — The Man Your Man Could Smell Like (2010), record grade A

fragments.text: Cravendale Cats with Thumbs: milk-obsessed cats evolve opposable thumbs and organize a society around that single development — a line of unnervingly calm cats flexing them as their owner realizes.
fragments.source: Cravendale — Cats with Thumbs (2011), record grade A

fragments.text: Samsung Ostrich: a flightless bird experiences flight through a VR headset, then carries the conviction into real air — a photoreal ostrich suspended against sunset, legs bicycling with astonished determination.
Levi's Odyssey: running bodies ignore architecture, bursting through wall after wall as if concrete were paper — two runners punch cleanly through an apartment and keep going into open forest air.
fragments.source: Levi's Engineered Jeans — Odyssey (2002), record grade B

fragments.text: IKEA Beds: a woman falls through a dream sky populated with floating beds, testing landing after landing until she reaches her own — thousands of beds suspended above the city.
Apple HomePod Welcome Home: music physically stretches and recolors a cramped apartment around its dancing tenant — a narrow hallway pulled open into impossible bands of space and color by her body.
fragments.source: Apple HomePod — Welcome Home (2018), record grade B

fragments.text: Burberry Open Spaces: garment freedom becomes gravityless group flight — four young people glide in formation over real British fields and coastline, their clothes moving as visibly as their bodies.
fragments.source: Burberry — Open Spaces (2021), record grade B

fragments.text: Kenzo World: a restrained gala guest's pent-up energy erupts into wild choreography through a formal building — she points down an empty hall and fires a green laser from her fingertip.
fragments.source: Kenzo — Kenzo World (2016), record grade C

fragments.text: Coca-Cola Masterpiece: one bottle relays from artwork to artwork, each world redrawing it in its own visual grammar — it changes medium at the exact seam where two famous canvases meet.
Honda Paper: the company's whole history unfolds in one hand-made paper universe — human hands flip, slide and rotate drawings so a motorcycle becomes car, racer, and finally flight, in camera.

Lacoste Timeless: every train carriage belongs to a different decade; a man's romantic pursuit stays continuous while clothes, passengers and design change — he crosses each coupling into a new era mid-stride.
Adidas_1 Hello Tomorrow: awakened shoes carry a sleeper through a dark gravity-shifting dream where each footstep generates the path — he runs across walls and ceiling on a trail of light.
fragments.source: Adidas_1 — Hello Tomorrow (2005), record grade C

fragments.text: PlayStation 2 The Third Place: the console is a portal into a monochrome dream realm of doubles and fragmented identity — a young man faces his doppelgänger beside an inexplicable human-duck figure.
fragments.source: PlayStation 2 — The Third Place (2000), record grade A

fragments.text: Honda The Other Side: one synchronized drive, two realities — holding the R key flips between a daytime school run and a nocturnal art-heist, matched frame for frame, gesture for gesture.
fragments.source: Honda Civic / Type R — The Other Side (2014), record grade A

fragments.text: Coca-Cola Happiness Factory: inside the vending machine lives a complete civilization whose creatures, terrain and machinery exist to prepare one bottle, which emerges through the coin slot after an ornate ritual.
fragments.source: Coca-Cola — Happiness Factory (2006), record grade B

fragments.text: Oreo Wonderfilled: sharing the cookie rewrites a story's emotional logic — an iconic antagonist receives an Oreo and the illustrated world around him blossoms into an unexpectedly friendly version of itself.
Sony Bravia Balls: hundreds of thousands of colored balls pour down real San Francisco streets — a whole hill filled edge-to-edge with bouncing color, the city still legible beneath the spectacle.
fragments.source: Sony Bravia — Balls (2005), record grade A

fragments.text: Guinness Surfer: the violence and white foam of a breaking wave materialize as enormous charging horses — mythic white stallions surge inside the dark wall of water beside the waiting surfers.
Cadbury Gorilla: the film performs pleasure instead of explaining chocolate — a solemn gorilla sits at a full drum kit, closes its eyes, and waits before the ecstatic musical release.
fragments.source: Cadbury Dairy Milk — Gorilla (2007), record grade A

fragments.text: Lurpak Kitchen Odyssey: making one omelette shot as an epic macro voyage — butter, egg and pan become monumental terrain and explosive weather while remaining appetizing food in close-up.
fragments.source: Lurpak — Kitchen Odyssey (2011), record grade B

fragments.text: Honda Cog: dozens of isolated car parts trigger one another in one exact chain reaction — each collision both payoff and setup — until the finished Accord rolls away.
fragments.source: Honda Accord — Cog (2003), record grade A

fragments.text: PlayStation 2 Mountain: thousands of people climb, support and overtake one another until their bodies form one colossal human mountain against the skyline, a single tiny figure reaching its summit.
Multiverse fall (Dr Strange MoM): two figures held center-frame while ~17 universes swap around them in one continuous 46-second camera plunge
Every universe boundary is a crystalline plane the camera pierces; the membrane breaks like glass with an impact whoosh
A red cape stays legible through every medium swap — one costume color as the thread of identity across visually noisy worlds
The falling couple is re-mediated mid-fall: 2D animation, then geometric voxels, then liquid paint — material state swapped while trajectory and silhouette hold
Amber honeycomb universe with giant bees: a world defined entirely by one material and its geometry, traversed in under two seconds
A contemporary street identical to ours but tilted diagonally — the cheapest universe: exactly one physics parameter wrong, everything else documentary
Two bodies as swirling liquid paint, still recognizably falling — identity carried by motion alone when material is gone
A blown-out white frame between worlds: over-exposure as the blank page before the next universe loads
Factory screen protector lifted by its green tab in one slow pull — static crackle, then mirror-perfect glass. The phone becomes yours the instant the film separates.
Laptop lid stratified with stickers, newest overlapping oldest; peeling one off lifts paint and leaves an adhesive ghost outlining where it lived.
Chrome vinyl wrap peeled off a car in one shoulder-wide sheet, factory paint emerging wet-looking underneath — a full-body molt performed for the camera.
Slime stretched slowly between both hands until it thins into a translucent membrane you can see the window through — one more inch and it tears.
Squishy bread miniature squeezed flat in a fist, then watched as its foam re-inflates in slow motion — a body that forgives every crush.
Hydro-dipping: paint film floats on water; a shoe pushed slowly through comes out wearing the surface. The membrane doesn't break — it transfers allegiance.
Fresh acrylic set filed to shape: ten hard shells glued over soft nails, hands suddenly someone older's. Weeks later they pop off with a hollow click.
Iron-on patch under a hot iron: adhesive melts, fabric fuses to denim, the jacket becomes a declared surface. First test afterward: pick at one corner.
Sneaker unboxing: tissue unfolded, deubré tags clipped, laces swapped before first wear. Lifting each shoe from its box is a decanting, performed for an audience.
Prying the case off a phone: for one naked minute the glass body is terrifyingly mortal, then a new shell clicks on and it's a different phone.
Temporary tattoo: press the wet cloth, count to thirty, peel the backing paper — the image has crossed onto your skin, already flaking at the edges by Friday.
AirPods case snapped shut mid-conversation: the magnetic click is a tiny door closing that everyone at the lunch table hears and understands.
Borrowed hoodie worn home: someone else's detergent smell becomes a second skin you can zip up, a person you can put on and refuse to return.
'You left the group.' One tap and a room of thirty people seals behind you — silent for you, audible to everyone still inside.
A story screenshotted, reposted, screenshotted again: each pass adds a frame inside a frame and a film of compression grain — gossip fossilizing in layers.
Kandi trade: strangers touch fingertips, then palms, then roll a beaded bracelet across the joined hands from one wrist to the other — membership transferred bead by bead.
The rare sticker kept on its backing paper for years because peeling it would spend it forever — the album's real collection is unspent potential.
Magnetic learner plate slapped on the family car: a provisional skin announcing an unfinished driver. The day the license arrives, it peels off in one motion.
Lady Bird mid-argument unbuckles and rolls out of the moving car — exiting her mother's world at speed because the door was the only available threshold.
Ben in his birthday scuba suit, POV through the mask: the parents' party muffled to bubbles as he sinks and stays on the pool floor, sealed in rubber.
Standing in the truck bed through the tunnel, arms out, radio up — walls compress, then the city opens: 'we are infinite' timed exactly to the exit.
fragments.source: The Perks of Being a Wallflower (2012)

fragments.text: Antoine escapes to a sea he has never seen, wades to the waterline, turns to camera — freeze-frame at the exact edge between water and whatever comes next.
Chihiro pulled by the hand through the dark tunnel; wind breathes from inside it like a throat. Beyond, her parents eat until they aren't parents anymore.
The car crosses Mexico as a sealed capsule: narration mutes the soundtrack to name the funerals and roadblocks passing outside windows the boys never really look through.
Mason packs the car for college while his mother sobs 'I just thought there would be more' — the leaving already finished before the engine starts.
Patrick sings 'Can't Take My Eyes Off You' down the stadium bleachers, marching band erupting from the stands — private feeling broadcast across every tier of seats.
The camera trails one student's back through school corridors for minutes, then time rewinds and follows another through the same passage — hallways as looping liminal circulation.
The veil goes on at every checkpoint and off at every party — one square of fabric toggling Marjane between selves at each border she crosses.
Graduation caps flung and held suspended mid-air above the crowd — four hundred uniforms shed at once, one shared weightless beat before they fall as litter.
Laney descends the staircase in a borrowed red dress, glasses gone: the reveal shot where a wardrobe change is filmed as a new person arriving.
